prequiz.title: How Client-Server Works
prequiz.subtitle: Key terms before you start
prequiz.description: Review these concepts before tackling the Client-Server quiz. Understanding them will help you answer questions about network architecture, DNS, scaling, proxies, and request flows.

keywords.definition: Any program or device that requests services or resources from a server. Common examples: web browsers, mobile apps, CLI tools making API calls.
A program or machine that listens on a network port and provides resources or services in response to client requests. Examples: web servers (Nginx, Apache), API servers, database servers.
The internet's phone book. Translates human-readable domain names (e.g., example.com) into IP addresses that network hardware uses to route traffic.
A numerical label assigned to each device on a network. IPv4 addresses look like 192.168.1.1; IPv6 addresses are longer. Used to route data to the correct destination.
A protocol where the server does not retain any memory of previous requests. Each request contains all the information needed to process it. HTTP is stateless.
A pattern that separates an application into: presentation tier (client/UI), application tier (business logic), and data tier (database). Each tier has a distinct responsibility.
A device or service that distributes incoming client requests across multiple server instances using algorithms like round-robin or least connections, improving availability and throughput.
Adding more identical server instances to a pool to handle increased load. Preferred for stateless web applications because it offers high availability and near-unlimited capacity.
Upgrading the hardware (CPU, RAM, storage) of an existing server. Simpler to implement but has a physical upper limit and creates a single point of failure.

A server that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them. Used for load balancing, SSL termination, caching, and hiding server infrastructure. Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, Cloudflare.
